to Nathan Spindel) Macintosh Computers in EECS Instructional Labs ---------------------------------------------- 30 MacPros (Intel x86 cpus) are in 200 Sutardja Dai Hall. 8 Mac G5s (PowerPC cpus) are in 349 Soda Hall. Software installed in

us) are in 349 Soda Hall. Software installed includes X11 Preview (Adobe Acrobat Reader) Gimp (open source image editing software) CHUD Microsoft Office for MacOSX Maya and Alias Xcode Please see http://inst.eecs.berkeley.edu/clients

Please see http://inst.eecs.berkeley.edu/clients for a list of computers, login and cardkey access information and maps of the rooms. Copying Files Between PCs and Macs ---------------------------------- The Instructional UNIX and MacOSX com

---------- The Instructional UNIX and MacOSX computers share the same password and home directory, but the Windows computers use an independent password and home directory. So you may find that you have a file in your UNIX/MacOSX home dire

that you have a file in your UNIX/MacOSX home directory that you would like to access from your Windows account, or vice versa. On each operating system, you can connect to the home directory of the other so that you can transfer files by d

f the other so that you can transfer files by dragging them between winodows. If you are on a Windows system: 1) Logon to your Windows account 2) Open the Start\Run window, enter "cmd" and click OK. 3) In the black command prompt wi

nd click OK. 3) In the black command prompt window, type: net use X: \\mamba\%USERNAME% /user:eecs\%USERNAME% /p:yes When prompted for a password, enter your Windows password. This assumes that you have the same USERNAME on t

ssumes that you have the same USERNAME on the UNIX systems as on the Windows systems. If you are on a MacOSX system: 1) Logon to your MacOS account (using your UNIX logon/password). (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos1.jpg)

word). (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos1.jpg) 2) In the Finder, click on the "Go" menu and click on "Connect to Server" or press the Command and "k" keys. (You are in the Finder application if the "Finder" menu is listed along t

tion if the "Finder" menu is listed along the top of the screen. If you are not in the Finder, you can start it by clicking on its icon in the Dock, which is a smiley face in a blue box along the bottom of the screen.) (Imag

e box along the bottom of the screen.) (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos2.jpg) 3) Type "smb://fileservice/class_name", where class_name is your class (such as "cs39j" or "cs294-7"). (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos3.jpg) 4) Enter "EE

age: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos3.jpg) 4) Enter "EECS" for the Domain and your Windows user name and password. Click on "Add to Favorites" so it appears automatically the next time. (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos4.jpg) 5) An icon with

/share/b/pub/jpg/macos4.jpg) 5) An icon with your class name appears on the desktop. Click on it. In the window that appears, click on the semester folder ("fa02" or etc) and click on the folder with your login name on it. That i

on the folder with your login name on it. That is your Windows home directory. (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos5.jpg) 6) To open a window with your UNIX/MacOS files, click on the disk drive icon with the local computer name on it an

icon with the local computer name on it and click on "Home". (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos5.jpg) 7) CS294-7 students can also connect to the group shared directory by connecting to "smb://mamba/cs294-7" in step 3 above (enter

ng to "smb://mamba/cs294-7" in step 3 above (enter Domain "EECS" and your Windows login and password if prompted). In the window that appears, click on the "public_html" folder, then the "group-share" folder to get to the group s

e "group-share" folder to get to the group shared directory. If you are on a UNIX system: You can transfer files to and from your Windows home directory using the Samba "smbclient" command. For an example, see http://ins

ent" command. For an example, see http://inst.eecs.berkeley.edu/connecting.html#file_transfers (UNIX to Windows). You could also use the CD-RW, the ZIP drives, Firewire drives (such as a Mac booted in Target mode), USB Drives or Memo

a Mac booted in Target mode), USB Drives or Memory Sticks on the Macs and PCs in 199 Cory, 330 Soda or 349 Soda and on the Solaris PCs in 275 Soda to transfer files to the removeable media. Finding Application Programs ----------------------

inding Application Programs ---------------------------- Some application programs are referenced as icons on the "Dock", a bar at the bottom of the MacOSX sreen. Placing the mouse pointer on an icon displays the name of the application. O

icon displays the name of the application. Other application programs can be found in a directory listing by clicking on "Applications" in any directory window. (Image: /share/b/pub/jpg/macos6.jpg) The "fink list" command lists all sofwar

6.jpg) The "fink list" command lists all sofware packages that have been installed. /Applications/FinkCommander/FinkCommander lists all sofware packages from http://fink.sourceforge.net/ and shows the ones that have been installed. (Sep 20

hows the ones that have been installed. (Sep 2005) On our Macs, the program /usr/sww/bin/AbiWord will read Microsoft Word Files. To run it: Start X11: /Applications/Utilities/X11 open an 'xterm' In the xterm, type /usr/sww/bin/AbiWord&

erm' In the xterm, type /usr/sww/bin/AbiWord& (July 2004) Microsoft Office for MacOSX is licensed for UC Berkeley-owned computers, including the Instructional student labs. EECS funds this via a campus site license agreement. (July 2

a a campus site license agreement. (July 2004) NeoOffice/J (www.neooffice.org) is a port of Sun's StarOffice productivity suite to Mac OS X. While it's described as a prototype, in fact it's quite stable. Among other things, it reads a

it's quite stable. Among other things, it reads and writes Microsoft Office format files. Working with XCode ------------------ Xcode does not like to save files to an NFS mounted drive (such as the home directories on our systems). Studen

as the home directories on our systems). Students must create a local folder and copy it between the Mac and the user home directory. They should remember to copy the files to their home directories when they are done so that they get the

ectories when they are done so that they get the same files when the move to a different system. Also, under Snow Leopard (MacOSX 10.6), XCode users need to be added to the _developer group. The sys admins ("inst@eecs.berkeley.edu") will do

he sys admins ("inst@eecs.berkeley.edu") will do this for all of the accounts in a class. Working with the iPhone STK --------------------------- The iPhone Simulator is typically installed in /Developer/STK on the Macs in 200 Sutardja-Dai Ha

Developer/STK on the Macs in 200 Sutardja-Dai Hall. It runs only on Intel-based Macs (i.e., not on our older PowerPC-based Mac G5s). The iPhone Simulator has to write some files to disk before it can launch, and it can't write them to NFS-

e it can launch, and it can't write them to NFS-mounted drives. The networked user home drives on the Instructional systems are NFS-mounted drives. So you need to create a symlink called "Iphone Simulator" in your home directory to a dir

hone Simulator" in your home directory to a directory in /tmp (which is on the local Mac computer drive) so that files are written to the local hard drive. For example, in a command line window, type these commands: mkdir /Users/Share

ndow, type these commands: mkdir /Users/Shared/$USER ln -s /Users/Shared/$USER "$HOME/Iphone Simulator" Don't forget to copy your files from the /Users/Shared/$USER directory back to your $HOME directory before you logout. The files

our $HOME directory before you logout. The files in /Users/Shared could be deleted after you have logged out. Here is the CS160 wiki page with more info on the iPhone simulator problem and some links: http://vis.berkeley.edu/courses/cs16

me links: http://vis.berkeley.edu/courses/cs160-sp10/wiki/index.php/How_to_fix_the_iPhone_development_environment_in_the_Orchard_Mac_Lab It appears that you can register for an iPhone Developer account for Apple App Store for free if it's

r account for Apple App Store for free if it's for instructional purposes: https://developer.apple.com/programs/ios/university/ Reading .dfg Files ------------------ MacOSX files that end in .dmg" are disk image files. At a Mac worska

nd in .dmg" are disk image files. At a Mac worskation console, you can access the contents simply by clicking on the file icon. At the MacOSX commmand line (such as if you are logged in using 'ssh'), you can access the contents by connecting t

h'), you can access the contents by connecting to the dmg like a disk drive, with the commands % hdiutil attach "An Example.dmg" /dev/disk1 /Volumes/An Example % ls "/Volumes/An Example" Accessing EECS UNIX files from the EECS Macs ----

Accessing EECS UNIX files from the EECS Macs -------------------------------------------- If you just want to share files between UNIX and MacOS systems, you can just store them in your home directory. The same home directory that you get on

ctory. The same home directory that you get on the Instuctional UNIX systems is your MacOSX home directory. If you want to execute programs on UNIX, open a Terminal window, type "ssh" and login to an Instructional UNIX computer (such as cory.

n to an Instructional UNIX computer (such as cory.eecs.berkeley.edu). For example, if you have the login 'jdoe' on the computer cory.eecs, type "ssh cory.eecs -l jdoe". If you want to run programs on UNIX that will pop up XWindows windows on

ams on UNIX that will pop up XWindows windows on the Mac: Start X11: /Applications/Utilities/X11 open an 'xterm' In the xterm, type ssh cory.eecs -X -l jdoe Accessing EECS Windows files from the EECS Macs ----------------

Windows files from the EECS Macs ----------------------------------------------- If you just want to share files between Windows and MacOS systems, you can connect to a Windows home directory from a Mac or to a UNIX/MacOSX home directory from

om a Mac or to a UNIX/MacOSX home directory from Windows using the steps described above in "Copying Files Between PCs and Macs". If you want to execute programs on Windows, you have to login to a Windows system (if you have an account). T

to a Windows system (if you have an account). There is no way to start a command shell on our Windows systems from another computer. Accessing EECS UNIX files from your Mac --------------------------------------- Pre-Mac0S X users can run S

-------------------- Pre-Mac0S X users can run Ssh (/share/b/pub/ssh.help) to login to UNIX systems and MacX (/share/b/pub/macx.help) to display XWindows graphics. MacOSX users can run Ssh (/share/b/pub/ssh.help) to login to UNIX systems. Pr

are/b/pub/ssh.help) to login to UNIX systems. Printing from the EECS Macs --------------------------- Start Print Center: /Applications/Utilities/"Print Center" Add Printer Choose in 1st selection Bar: IP Printing Choose Printer Type: LPD/L

tion Bar: IP Printing Choose Printer Type: LPD/LPR Choose Printer Address: iprint.eecs.berkeley.edu Choose Queue Name: lw349, lw199 or lw105 You can have 1 printer definition for each printer Once you choose a default printer, You can pri

Once you choose a default printer, You can print to it from a terminal window using regular unix commands, such as: enscript -r -f Courier28 File Microsoft Word and the Library/Fonts Folder ------------------------------------------- To

------------------------------------------- To avoid filling your Library/Fonts Folder, which counts against your quota: In a Terminal Window: cd Library/ rm -rf Fonts ln -s /Applications/"Microsoft Office 2004"/Office/Fonts Fonts For

s/"Microsoft Office 2004"/Office/Fonts Fonts Forcing Safari to not cache files in your Home Folder ---------------------------------------------------- Since all the macs are running the squid proxy server, which caches web files to the local

oxy server, which caches web files to the local disk, you don't need browser caching. Use /Applications/"Safari Enhancer" to disable the cache, which would count against your quota. Terminal Commands to open the cdrom tray: -----------------

Commands to open the cdrom tray: ----------------------------------------- drutil tray open drutil tray eject MacOS Tips ---------- Symptom: "I get the error message 'unknown parameter "xterm-color"' when I ssh from a Mac to a UNIX syste

m-color"' when I ssh from a Mac to a UNIX system and run an XWindows command." Solution: Type "setenv TERM xterm" when you log to the UNIX system. Or, on the Mac, go to the Terminal App "Preferences" window and select a different kind of t

erences" window and select a different kind of term. Special keyboard function: <3> = screen image to picture#.tif file in home dir = switch amongst open applications = on a folder name, opens folder in a new window = quit the current applicatio

lder in a new window = quit the current application <.> = quit the current application = quit the current application = during boot, until 2nd sound, resets PRAM main power button = press for 5 sec to force shutdown = on icon in the Dock, allows "

to force shutdown = on icon in the Dock, allows "Keep in Dock" = on icon in the Dock, allows "Force Quit" = on an iMovie clip, keeps orginal clip on shelf = open CD tray Useful WEB sites: http://macenterprise.org - university/enterprise l

tp://macenterprise.org - university/enterprise labs http://www.macosxlabs.org - university labs http://www.apple.com/macosx - downlaods, tech support http://www.info.apple.com/usen/macosx - FAQs, upgrades http://www.info.apple.c

acosx - FAQs, upgrades http://www.info.apple.com/usen/security - Apple Product Security http://discussions.info.apple.com - bulletin board http://www.macworld.com - magazine http://www.macosxhints.com - Q&A, shareware, freeware

www.macosxhints.com - Q&A, shareware, freeware http://www.versiontracker.com - free and commercial software http://www.macintouch.com - Mac news and info http://www.ilenesmachine.com - technical info http://magnet.berkeley.edu

- technical info http://magnet.berkeley.edu - UC Berkeley Mac Admin Group http://ioxperts.com - device drivers for MacOS MacOSX Tips for CS3 (thanks to Nathan Spindel) ---------------------------------------------- | Connecting from

----------------------------- | Connecting from Home - for those running Mac OS X 10.2 or 10.3 | | Instead of going to lab, you can connect to the lab computers remotely. | You have the option of SSH'ing (secure shell) into the server and usin

of SSH'ing (secure shell) into the server and using | the command line interface (similar to using the xterm in lab), or you | can remotely run the X programs on your machine (like emacs and Mozilla). | I only recommend running the X programs r

la). | I only recommend running the X programs remotely if you have a fast | internet connection, otherwise it will just be painful. | | To connect via CLI (command line interface): | | OS X comes pre-installed with an ssh client, so you do

comes pre-installed with an ssh client, so you do not need to download | or install anything. To connect using the ssh client, do the following: | | 1) Open Terminal (it's in /Applications/Utilities/Terminal) | 2) At the prompt type: ssh -l |

ties/Terminal) | 2) At the prompt type: ssh -l | For instance, I use: ssh -l cs61a-ny h30.cs.berkeley.edu | My username is cs61a-ny and h30.cs.berkeley.edu is the cs3 lab server. | 3) Use the password that you normally use to login to the la

password that you normally use to login to the lab machines. | 4) Now that you've logged into the cs3 server, you can use CLI utilities | like STk, directory/file utilities (ls, mv, cp, rm, etc.), and text | editors (vi, pico, emacs, etc.

.), and text | editors (vi, pico, emacs, etc.) | 5) When you are done, type exit (or control-d) to log out. | | To remotely access X utilities: | | OSX does not come with a XFree86 server pre-installed, which is what you | need to be ab

pre-installed, which is what you | need to be able to host X applications. Luckily, Apple has released one | for free. It comes with OS X 10.3 (you may need to install it from your | third install CD), or you can download it here. These instruc

ll CD), or you can download it here. These instructions assume | you've installed Apple's X11 software: | | 1) Open X11 (it's in /Applications/Utilities/X11) | In Aplle's X11 OPTION-Click simulates the Mouse's Middle Button | In Aplle's X11 CO

tes the Mouse's Middle Button | In Aplle's X11 COMMAND-Click simulates the Mouse's Right Button | 2) An xterm will open. | At the prompt, type: ssh -X -C -l | The -X flag enables X11 connection forwarding, and the -C flag enables | com

ion forwarding, and the -C flag enables | compression (this makes it slightly faster). | 3) Use the password that you normally use to login to the lab machines. | 4) Now that you've logged into the cs61a/cs3 server, you can use the CLI |

the cs61a/cs3 server, you can use the CLI | utilities as well as X utilities. For instance, typing 'xlogo &' will | start an instance of xlogo in the background. Now you can use emacs, | and the STk modeler, and any other X apps that

and the STk modeler, and any other X apps that h30 has installed. | 5) When you are done, type exit (or control-d) to log out. | | | To get STk and emacs to work together locally: | | Check here for downloads and instructions to get STk r

k here for downloads and instructions to get STk running on OSX. Note | that you must install the STk folder in /Applications/. In order to get | emacs and STk running together nicely, I had to do a little modification. | This is what I did: |

a little modification. | This is what I did: | | 1) Create an alias at /usr/bin/stk like so: | Type the following three lines in a Terminal window: | echo /Applications/STk/bin/snow -l \ /Applications/STk/lib/stk/site-scheme/berkeley.

\ /Applications/STk/lib/stk/site-scheme/berkeley.scm > stk | chmod +x stk | sudo mv stk /usr/bin/ (use your normal login password when it prompts you) | | 2) Add the following three lines to your ~/.emacs file: | | (setq scheme-prog

to your ~/.emacs file: | | (setq scheme-program-name "stk") | (setq auto-mode-alist (cons '("\\.stk\\'" . scheme-mode) auto-mode-alist)) | (add-hook 'inferior-scheme-mode-hook (lambda () (split-window))) | | 3) Now, in emacs, type 'M

(split-window))) | | 3) Now, in emacs, type 'M-x run-scheme' to get STk running in a new frame. | (To invoke M-x, hit the escape key, let go of it, and hit the x key). | To see the STk/emacs integration options, give the STk frame focus

macs integration options, give the STk frame focus and | check out the Scheme menu in the emacs menubar or check out Colin Dewey's | quick tutorial (note: M-s should not work). | | | Tips | | If you've grown accustomed to the wonder

ps | | If you've grown accustomed to the wonderful Sun keyboards, which have the | control key where the caps lock (which in my opinion, is useless) key on | Apple's keyboards is, check out uControl, which lets you remap your keys. | I've fo

ntrol, which lets you remap your keys. | I've found it to be quite useful. | | You can run emacs (in X) and STk locally, meaning that they will reside on | your computer. This will enable you to use them when you are not connected | to the i

o use them when you are not connected | to the internet, and the utilities will be more responsive than running them | remotely, as described above. The version of emacs that comes with OSX is a | CLI-only version, and if you want to run the X

| CLI-only version, and if you want to run the X component you'll need to | download and install it. I highly suggest the Fink project, a utility that | enables you to quickly download and install many pre-compiled X and CLI unix | utilities.

l many pre-compiled X and CLI unix | utilities. | | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | Written by Nathan Spindel, March 18th 2003. | Modified October 16th 2003. | Please send questions or comments
16th 2003. | Please send questions or comments to nspindel@uclink.berkeley.edu. EECS Instructional Support Group 384/386 Cory, 333 Soda inst@eecs.berkeley.edu
